text:
Overview
AWS security rests on the shared responsibility model: AWS secures the cloud
infrastructure, the customer secures configurations, identity, and data within it. Strong
postures combine tight IAM, continuous detection (GuardDuty/Security Hub), and
encryption/network controls enforced through Organizations-wide guardrails.
IAM Core Concepts
Concept
Details
IAM Policy
JSON document granting/denying actions on resources; identity-
based or resource-based
Role
Assumable identity with temporary credentials — preferred over
long-lived IAM user keys
IAM Identity Center
Centralized SSO across AWS Organizations accounts (successor to
AWS SSO)
SCP (Service Control
Policy)
Org-level permission guardrail — sets the maximum boundary,
doesn't itself grant access
Permission
Boundary
Caps the maximum permissions an IAM identity can have, even if its
policy grants more
Trust Policy
Defines who/what can assume a role (cross-account, federated,
service)
Detection & Monitoring Services

policy grants more
Trust Policy
Defines who/what can assume a role (cross-account, federated,
service)
Detection & Monitoring Services
GuardDuty — ML-based threat detection over CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, DNS logs; no
agents required
Security Hub — aggregates findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, third-party
tools against CIS/AWS FSBP benchmarks
CloudTrail — API call audit log; enable multi-region, organization trail, log-file integrity
validation
C L O U D C H E AT S H E E T · AW S
AWS Security Fundamentals
IAM, detection, and workload protection across the AWS Well-Architected security pillar
• 
• 
• 
Mazhar Khan — Cloud Security Portfolio
Page 1 of 3

AWS Config — continuous resource configuration recording and compliance rule
evaluation
Inspector — automated vulnerability scanning for EC2, ECR images, and Lambda
functions
Macie — ML-based discovery and classification of sensitive data in S3
Network & Data Protection
Security Groups — stateful, instance-level virtual firewall (allow rules only)
NACLs — stateless, subnet-level; supports explicit deny, evaluated in rule-number order
VPC endpoints (Gateway/Interface) — keep traffic to S3/DynamoDB/other services
off the public internet
KMS — centralized key management; CMKs with defined key policies and automatic/
manual rotation
S3 Block Public Access — account/bucket-level setting that should be enabled by
default org-wide
AWS WAF + Shield — L7 filtering and DDoS protection in front of ALB/CloudFront/API
Gateway
Landing Zone / Multi-Account Pattern
AWS Organizations root → OUs (Security, Infrastructure, Workloads) → SCP guardrails per OU →

Gateway
Landing Zone / Multi-Account Pattern
AWS Organizations root → OUs (Security, Infrastructure, Workloads) → SCP guardrails per OU →
dedicated Log Archive and Security Tooling accounts receive aggregated CloudTrail/Config/GuardDuty
findings, isolated from workload accounts to preserve audit integrity even if a workload account is
compromised.
Well-Architected Security Pillar — Design Principles
Implement a strong identity foundation — least privilege, centralized identity,
eliminate long-term credentials where possible
Enable traceability — log and monitor, integrate alerts with automated response
Apply security at all layers — edge network, VPC, subnet, load balancer, instance, OS,
application, code
Automate security best practices — codify controls as scalable, repeatable IaC
Prepare for security events — incident response playbooks, game days, forensics-
ready account design

Common Pitfalls
Pitfall
Fix
Long-lived IAM user
access keys
Prefer roles with temporary STS credentials; enforce key rotation/
expiry where keys are unavoidable
Single-account
sprawl with no
guardrails
Adopt AWS Organizations + SCPs across a multi-account landing
zone
CloudTrail enabled in
one region only
Enable an organization trail covering all regions with log-file
validation
Overly permissive
S3 bucket policies
Enable Block Public Access by default; audit with Access Analyzer
GuardDuty findings
reviewed manually
and late
Route findings through Security Hub into EventBridge-driven auto-
remediation
Interview / Exam Quick Hits
SCPs never grant permissions — they only restrict the maximum available permission
set for an account
GuardDuty findings feed automatically into Security Hub and can trigger 
EventBridge-driven auto-remediation
Security Groups: stateful (return traffic auto-allowed); NACLs: stateless (must allow
both directions explicitly)

EventBridge-driven auto-remediation
Security Groups: stateful (return traffic auto-allowed); NACLs: stateless (must allow
both directions explicitly)
Cross-account access should use IAM roles with external ID, not shared long-lived
credentials
S3 bucket policies + IAM policies are evaluated together — an explicit Deny in either
always wins
